itineraries.title: Essential Peru Journey: Lima to Machu Picchu
itineraries.subtitle: From Lima to the Paracas cliffs, from Huacachina's dunes to Nazca's geoglyphs, from Arequipa's white streets to Cusco's Inca stones and Machu Picchu.
itineraries.notes: Book a domestic flight Arequipa-Cusco (D8→D9). The Ollantaytambo-Aguas Calientes leg is exclusively by train. Reserve Huayna Picchu or Montaña Machu Picchu in advance (strict quotas). Account for altitude in Cusco (11,150 ft / 3,400 m): plan a half-day acclimatization upon arrival.

map_keywords: Puente de los Suspiros Barranco Lima

visits.text: Drop your bags and head straight down to the Malecón: the cliffside promenade runs along Miraflores' edge 230 feet above the Pacific, and it's the perfect antidote to jet lag. Walk toward Parque del Amor—couples gather around Víctor Delfín's sculpture El Beso (The Kiss)—and watch surfers tackle the Costa Verde waves below. Lima is the only South American capital with direct ocean access, and from these cliffs you grasp it instantly. Continue north toward Barranco to stretch your legs.

In the heart of Miraflores, Huaca Pucllana rises between modern high-rises: a 15-acre adobe pyramid built by the Lima culture between 200 and 700 CE. The night tours are particularly striking—the site is illuminated and the terraces stand out sharply against the city darkness. Opt for the guided visit, which includes access to the small on-site museum. Allow 1.5 hours.

activities.text: In the evening, head to Barranco, Lima's bohemian quarter, about fifteen minutes on foot or by taxi from Miraflores. Cross the Bridge of Sighs, stroll down Bajada de Baños—the pedestrian avenue that descends toward the beach—and admire the street art murals covering the walls of old colonial mansions. Barranco has the highest concentration of artistic graffiti in Lima. The bars come alive around 8 pm: perfect for your first pisco sour.

visits.text: Arrive at Plaza Mayor around noon: the guards parade in front of the Government Palace to the sound of El Cóndor Pasa, pigeons scatter with every drum roll, and the ochre-yellow facades gleam under the Pacific light. This is where Pizarro founded Lima on January 18, 1535—the baroque cathedral across the square houses his tomb. The historic center was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1991. Walk up the pedestrian street Jirón de la Unión to reach Plaza San Martín, less touristy and more lively on weekdays.

A stone's throw from Plaza Mayor, the San Francisco convent is one of the best-preserved baroque buildings in Latin America. The current structure was consecrated in 1672, after an earthquake destroyed its predecessor in 1655. Don't miss the underground catacombs: used as a cemetery until 1808, they hold the remains of approximately 25,000 people, with bones arranged in geometric patterns in pits. The guided tour lasts about an hour—photography is forbidden inside.

The Larco Museum, in the Pueblo Libre district, occupies an 18th-century viceregal mansion built atop a 7th-century pre-Columbian pyramid. Founded in 1926 by archaeologist Rafael Larco Hoyle, it holds over 45,000 pieces: Moche ceramics, Paracas textiles, Chimú goldwork. The pre-Columbian erotic gallery—a collection of Moche pottery illustrating these civilizations' ritual practices—is among the world's most famous. The bougainvillea gardens provide a pleasant setting for a break. The museum stays open until 10 pm—convenient for avoiding morning crowds. Allow at least 2 hours.

activities.text: Before or after the Larco Museum, stop by Parque Kennedy in the heart of Miraflores: the park's cats have become a local institution. Artisans set up on weekends. It's also the ideal spot to try picarones—Peruvian doughnuts made with sweet potato and served with molasses sauce—from street vendors.

map_keywords: Puerto El Chaco Paracas bateaux Ballestas

visits.text: En route to the Ballestas Islands, the boat skirts the peninsula and passes the Paracas Candelabra—a 590-foot-tall geoglyph etched into a dune, attributed to the Paracas people over 2,000 years ago and visible only from the sea. Book your excursion the day before and choose the first morning departure (around 8 am): the sea is calmer and the wildlife more active. On the islands' rocks, you'll spot colonies of South American sea lions, Humboldt penguins—the only penguin species in Peru—Peruvian pelicans, Guanay cormorants, and Peruvian boobies. You don't land on the islands: everything is viewed from the boat. The tour lasts about 2 hours.

In the afternoon, head to Paracas National Reserve: it's one of the few places on Earth where desert meets Pacific Ocean head-on. Playa Roja owes its color to fragments of red volcanic rock—the contrast with the blue sea is striking. Note that the formation nicknamed "La Catedral," an emblematic rock arch of the reserve, collapsed in a 2007 earthquake. Catch the sunset from the cliffs.

activities.text: Back at the small port of El Chaco, settle onto a terrace to sample ceviche made with freshly caught fish. Paracas Bay is one of the richest fishing grounds in the South Pacific, with over 180 documented fish species.

visits.text: Huacachina is a fascinating geographical anomaly: a natural lagoon ringed by palm trees, lost amid dunes rising over 330 feet. The place is so iconic it appears on Peru's 50-sol banknote. Walk around the oasis on arrival, before the heat sets in—midday temperatures hover around 86°F (30°C). Swimming in the lagoon is not recommended.

activities.text: The signature activity in Huacachina is dune buggy rides and sandboarding, ideally in late afternoon to catch the golden sunset light. Professional drivers take you hurtling across the dune crests in 4x4 buggies—buckle up tight. Stops let you race down the slopes on sandboards: standing for the athletic, lying on your stomach for everyone else (faster and just as fun). Book at any agency in the oasis for a departure around 4 pm.

The Ica region is Peru's main wine and pisco zone. Bodegas offer tours and tastings a few miles from the oasis. It's a chance to understand how pisco—grape brandy fermented in clay jars—is produced using methods handed down since colonial times.

map_keywords: Aéroport de Nazca survol lignes géoglyphes

visits.text: The Nazca geoglyphs were etched into the desert between 200 BCE and 600 CE by the Nazca culture: they span 190 square miles and include over 800 straight lines, 300 geometric figures, and 70 depictions of animals and plants. Designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1994, they truly reveal themselves only from the air. The small-plane overflight lasts 30 to 35 minutes: the pilot banks the aircraft alternately to each side so every passenger sees the hummingbird, the spider, the monkey, the condor, and the astronaut. Arrive early in the morning—desert winds pick up by midday, and turbulence with them. If you're prone to motion sickness, take preventive medication. Ground alternative: the metal viewing tower along the Pan-American Highway offers glimpses of the tree and the hands from ground level.

The Chauchilla Cemetery, 19 miles from Nazca, is one of Peru's most striking archaeological sites: mummies over 1,000 years old rest in open-air tombs in the desert. Thanks to the extremely dry climate, they're remarkably preserved—hair, teeth, and funerary textiles are still visible. The site was looted before archaeological protection; the mummies have since been returned to their original graves. The visit is not suitable for young children. Arrange an organized excursion from Nazca that includes transport and a guide.

activities.text: Two options for reaching Arequipa. Option 1: overnight bus from Nazca (departs around 8–9 pm, arrives Arequipa around 6 am)—comfortable with major companies, and you save a hotel night. Option 2: day drive the next morning (~355 miles, ~9 hours on the Pan-American South). If you choose the night bus, book a cama seat (reclining sleeper) for a decent night's rest.

map_keywords: Quartier San Lázaro Arequipa ruelles sillar

visits.text: Arriving in Arequipa from Nazca, something shifts in the air—altitude climbs to 7,660 feet (2,335 m), the desert's dry heat gives way to sharper, cooler light. Drop your bags and head straight to Plaza de Armas: everything is white. The buildings, the arcades, the cathedral—all carved from sillar, the volcanic stone quarried from Chachani's flanks, which earned the city its nickname "The White City" and its UNESCO listing in 2000. Look north from the square: El Misti (19,101 ft / 5,822 m) rises above the rooftops with its near-perfect cone. Settle onto a terrace and let the city come to you.

Push on to the San Lázaro neighborhood, half a mile from the Plaza: it's Arequipa's oldest quarter, predating even the city's official founding in 1540. Step into Violín alley or Ripacha alley—passages so narrow shoulders touch the walls—and you'll stumble onto white sillar houses with wrought-iron grilles and flower-filled courtyards visible through half-open doorways. The quarter is nearly deserted on weekdays: wander without a map—it's the only way to truly see it.

The Yanahuara viewpoint, 1.25 miles from downtown, offers Arequipa's most famous panoramic view. Ten sillar arches built in the 19th century, engraved with verses by Arequipeño poets, frame the silhouettes of El Misti (19,101 ft / 5,822 m) and Chachani (19,872 ft / 6,057 m). Go in late afternoon for sunset light over the white city. Admission is free.

activities.text: Arequipa is considered Peru's gastronomic capital. In the evening, seek out a traditional picantería in the Yanahuara district to try rocoto relleno (stuffed spicy pepper with meat and eggs), chupe de camarones (river prawn chowder), or adobo (marinated pork stew). The San Camilo market, liveliest in the morning, lets you discover local products at any hour.

map_keywords: Museo Santuarios Andinos momie Juanita Arequipa

visits.text: Santa Catalina Monastery is Arequipa's most striking attraction: founded September 10, 1579, by Doña María de Guzmán, a wealthy widow who surrendered all her possessions to live there in seclusion, it remained hermetically sealed from the outside world for nearly 400 years. Its doors opened to the public only in 1970. At 215,000 square feet, it's a city within the city: streets named after Spanish towns (Córdoba, Sevilla, Burgos), cloisters with red and orange walls, nuns' cells with individual kitchens, communal laundry. Today, a few dozen nuns still live in the complex's northern wing. Allow at least 2.5 hours—a guide is highly recommended for the first section, then explore freely.

The Museo Santuarios Andinos houses the mummy Juanita, discovered in September 1995 by anthropologist Johan Reinhard and Peruvian guide Miguel Zárate during an expedition on Nevado Ampato (20,702 ft / 6,310 m). This Inca girl aged 12 to 15 was sacrificed as part of the Capacocha ritual, likely to appease the gods after local volcanic eruptions around 1440–1450. Preserved by the cold at over 19,700 feet for 500 years, she's displayed in a refrigerated case at -13°F (-25°C). In 2023, a Polish-Peruvian team reconstructed her face in 3D. The visit lasts about 30 minutes and begins with an introductory film. Photography is forbidden inside.

activities.text: In late afternoon, walk up to the Church of the Company of Jesus, whose churrigueresque baroque facade is one of the most ornate in South America. It borders Plaza de Armas on the east side. Step inside to see the interior and its murals.

map_keywords: Marché San Camilo Arequipa produits locaux

visits.text: Take a taxi to the Sabandía countryside, 5 miles southeast: the road descends into an irrigated valley where corn, alfalfa, and olive trees grow, with El Misti as a backdrop—the contrast with Nazca's desert two days ago is striking. At the end of the track, the colonial Sabandía mill dates from the 17th century; restored and still functioning, it grinds grain the old-fashioned way. Cows graze around it, Inca irrigation channels run between the terraces. If you like walking, the path along the Socabaya river offers an hour's peaceful stroll away from tour groups.

Back in town, stop by the San Camilo market for final purchases of local products: Andean aromatic herbs, cheeses, sweet specialties including queso helado (not cheese but ice cream flavored with coconut, cinnamon, and cloves, despite its name).

activities.text: Late in the day, flight Arequipa → Cusco (~1 hour). Rodriguez Ballon Airport is 5 miles from downtown. Enjoy the volcano views from the window on takeoff—El Misti with its perfect cone is unmistakable.

visits.text: The moment you step off the plane, you feel it: the air is thinner at 11,150 feet (3,400 m). Climbing the airport stairs leaves you slightly breathless, your temples might start to throb. Don't worry—it's normal and passes in a few hours if you don't push yourself. Order a mate de coca as soon as you reach your hotel (coca leaf tea genuinely helps ease altitude sickness) and reserve this first afternoon for gentle strolling on Plaza de Armas. The Cathedral and the Church of the Company, built on Inca foundations, border the square—look at the lower stones: they're Inca, the upper ones, Spanish. Don't rush yet.

Qorikancha—"golden enclosure" in Quechua—was the Inca Empire's most important temple, the spiritual center dedicated to Inti (the Sun). According to Spanish chroniclers, its walls were covered with 700 sheets of gold and its gardens held sculptures of animals and plants in precious metal. In 1533–1534, the conquistadors looted the gold to pay Atahualpa's ransom, then gave the site to the Dominicans who built Santo Domingo convent on its foundations. The original Inca walls withstood every earthquake—including the 1950 quake—while the colonial convent collapsed with each tremor. This contrast between the two architectures remains perfectly visible today.

activities.text: In the evening, wander the artisan quarter of San Blas, north of Plaza de Armas. Its cobbled lanes and craft workshops (silversmiths, potters, weavers) come alive in late afternoon. Finish at the San Pedro market to try fresh fruit juices and local cheeses.

visits.text: Sacsayhuamán lies 1.25 miles north of Plaza de Armas at 12,140 feet (3,700 m) altitude. This Inca complex, built at Pachacutec's initiative in the 15th century and completed under Huayna Capac, consists of three parallel zigzag ramparts stretching 1,970 feet. Its monolithic blocks weigh between 128 and 200 tons for the largest—assembled without mortar with precision that won't admit a sheet of paper. The Spanish dismantled stones to build Cusco's churches. You can reach the site on foot from the Plaza in 30 to 45 minutes, or by taxi. Arrive before 10 am to avoid tour groups.

On the same circuit, a few miles from Sacsayhuamán, Qenqo is an Inca ceremonial center carved directly from a rock outcrop: its underground galleries and sculpted channels probably served for noble mummification rituals. The Quechua word means "labyrinth." Tambomachay, farther on, is a complex of ritual channels and fountains whose springs still flow today—the Incas possessed remarkable hydraulic mastery. Both sites are included in the Cusco tourist ticket (Boleto Turístico).

activities.text: In the afternoon, return to San Blas to explore the artisan workshops in more detail. The San Pedro market is ideal late in the day for shopping: alpaca textiles, ceramics, Andean medicinal herbs. Plan your dinner in the historic quarter—Cusco cuisine offers local dishes like cuy al horno (roast guinea pig) or lomo saltado.

visits.text: En route to Ollantaytambo, first stop at Pisac: the Inca ruins perched on the heights overlook a valley of startling vastness. Take time to climb to the Temple of the Sun for the panoramic view. Below, Pisac's artisan market is one of the region's best-stocked—textiles, ceramics, silver jewelry.

The circular terraces of Moray constitute one of Peru's most original archaeological curiosities: three concentric amphitheaters carved into the earth, up to 720 feet in diameter, reaching depths of 100 feet. The temperature difference between bottom and rim exceeds 27°F (15°C)—the Incas likely used them as an agricultural laboratory to acclimate different species to different altitudes. A few miles away, the Maras salt pans align over 3,000 white salt pools on a mountainside, exploited since Inca times. The visual contrast is spectacular.

Ollantaytambo is doubly remarkable: its Inca fortress with monumental terraces and unfinished Temple of the Sun (the Spanish halted construction in 1536) ranks among the best-preserved military-religious complexes in the Andes. But the town itself is unique in Peru: its narrow streets and irrigation channels date from Inca times and are still inhabited today—the country's only living Inca village.

activities.text: Book your train Ollantaytambo → Aguas Calientes in advance (several weeks in high season). The ride (~1.5 hours) follows the Urubamba River as it narrows progressively into a jungle gorge—the atmosphere changes completely. Dinner in Ollantaytambo at a restaurant facing the illuminated fortress.

map_keywords: Gare de train Aguas Calientes Machu Picchu
Aguas Calientes village Machu Picchu Pueblo
Navettes bus Machu Picchu Aguas Calientes

visits.text: The train deposits you in Aguas Calientes into another world: the jungle has taken over, slopes are cloaked in dense humid vegetation, and the Urubamba River roars below restaurant windows. Steam rises from the hot springs that gave the village its name. It's loud, damp, hemmed-in—and it's perfect, because it announces something. Collect your Machu Picchu entry ticket if you haven't already (kiosks are available at the ticket office opposite the station), and scout the shuttle queue for tomorrow: the first buses leave at 5:30 am.

activities.text: The natural hot springs (Aguas Calientes owes its name to the thermal waters) are a 10-minute walk from the station. After long days of travel and sightseeing, it's ideal relaxation before the Machu Picchu stage. Turn in early: tomorrow's wake-up is before 5 am.

map_keywords: Temple des Trois Fenêtres Machu Picchu

visits.text: Take the shuttle bus from 5:30 am onward from Aguas Calientes: the winding ascent takes 25 minutes to the citadel entrance. Machu Picchu was rediscovered by American explorer Hiram Bingham on July 24, 1911, after local farmers directed him to the site. Designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1983, it ranks among the New Seven Wonders of the World. The main circuit covers the Intihuatana (the Inca stone "sundial"), the Temple of the Three Windows, the Mortars Quarter, and the cascading agricultural terraces. A local guide is strongly recommended for understanding the site's organization. Allow 3 to 4 hours for a complete visit.

For those who booked in advance: Huayna Picchu (quota of 400 people/day) is the vertiginous climb up the iconic peak that overlooks the citadel—the summit view is breathtaking but the ascent is steep. Less athletic but equally spectacular alternative: Montaña Machu Picchu (quota of 800 people/day), higher at 10,111 feet (3,082 m) but with a steadier slope, offers an even more panoramic overview of the citadel.

activities.text: Return late afternoon: shuttle bus down to Aguas Calientes, then train to Ollantaytambo (departures between 4 pm and 7:30 pm depending on company), and finally minibus or taxi from Ollantaytambo to Cusco (~1.5 hours). Evening arrival in Cusco. Final dinner in the San Blas quarter.

practical_info.booking: Book return train at the same time as outbound

map_keywords: Aéroport Alejandro Velasco Astete Cusco vol Lima
Quartier San Blas Cusco artisans derniers achats

visits.text: Last morning in Cusco before the return flight. Use it to walk through San Blas once more: the artisan workshops open early. The San Pedro market, two steps from Plaza de Armas, is the best place for final purchases—alpaca textiles, Cusco ceramics, Andean spices. The Cusco → Lima flight takes about 1 hour 20 minutes.

activities.text: Allow at least 2 hours before your flight: Alejandro Velasco Astete Airport is 15 minutes from downtown by taxi but security checks take time. On takeoff, look out the window on the west side: El Misti, Chachani, the snow-capped ridges—the same panorama as arriving from Arequipa, except now you know what lies behind them. An hour twenty later, Lima and the Pacific Ocean. The circuit is complete: from coast to Andes and back to the coast, in two weeks and 14 days of a Peru unlike anywhere else.

seo.h1_title: Essential Peru Journey: Lima to Machu Picchu
seo.intro_paragraph: Two weeks to cross Peru from the Pacific coast to the Andes: Lima with its colonial catacombs, the Ballestas Islands and their penguins, the Huacachina oasis amid the dunes, the Nazca geoglyphs viewed from a small plane, Arequipa the white city with its Inca mummy and colorful streets, then Cusco and the Sacred Valley all the way to Machu Picchu.
seo.seo_hook: Geoglyphs visible from the sky, Inca stones fitted without mortar, a citadel lost in the clouds, an oasis in the desert: a journey crossing 5,000 years of Peruvian history.
featured_list: Machu Picchu: Inca citadel rediscovered by Hiram Bingham in 1911
Nazca Lines: geoglyphs from 200 BCE viewed by plane
Santa Catalina Monastery: city within a city, closed to the public for 400 years
Huacachina: natural oasis pictured on Peru's 50-sol banknote

places_taglines.PE::lima: Capital founded in 1535 where colonial catacombs meet Pacific cliffs and Barranco street art
places_taglines.PE::paracas: Where desert plunges into the Pacific and Humboldt penguins share rocky islands with sea lions
places_taglines.PE::huacachina: A palm-fringed lagoon lost amid towering dunes and sunset sandboarding runs
places_taglines.PE::nazca: Ancient geoglyphs etched across the desert floor, visible only from the sky since 200 BCE
places_taglines.PE::arequipa: The White City carved from volcanic stone, where a monastery hid from the world for four centuries
places_taglines.PE::cusco: Where Inca stonework shoulders Spanish churches at 11,000 feet above the clouds
places_taglines.PE::ollantaytambo: Peru's only living Inca village, where cobbled streets still follow 500-year-old canals
places_taglines.PE::aguas-calientes: The jungle gateway to Machu Picchu, where thermal springs rise between cloud forest peaks

highlights: Overflight of Nazca geoglyphs (200 BCE–600 CE) designated UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1994
Santa Catalina Monastery in Arequipa: 215,000 sq ft opened to the public only since 1970
Machu Picchu rediscovered by Hiram Bingham on July 24, 1911, UNESCO listed in 1983
Sacsayhuamán: stone blocks weighing 128 to 200 tons assembled without mortar

itineraries.title: The Grand Southern Peru Circuit: Coast, Andes & Machu Picchu
itineraries.subtitle: From Lima's coastal cliffs to the Ica dunes, from the depths of Colca Canyon to Lake Titicaca at 3,812m, all the way to Cusco and Machu Picchu—21 days through the heart of Andean civilization.
itineraries.notes: High-altitude travel: acclimatization is essential at Puno (3,812m) and Cusco (3,400m). Plan for 1-2 days of light activity upon arrival in these cities. Advance booking required for Huayna Picchu/Montaña Machu Picchu (400/day quota). The Chivay→Puno route goes direct WITHOUT backtracking through Arequipa.

visits.text: Lima is a city of 11 million people sprawling through the coastal fog facing the Pacific—and the Plaza Mayor is where it all began. Pizarro founded the city right here on January 18, 1535, and the bronze fountain at the center of the square dates from 1650: it's been standing since Louis XIV was a child. Take your time and turn slowly on your heel. On one side, the Government Palace, where the changing of the guard happens daily at noon to the sound of El Cóndor Pasa. Across from it, the 17th-century baroque cathedral, rebuilt multiple times after earthquakes—Francisco Pizarro himself is buried inside. The atmosphere in late afternoon, when the plaza fills with Limeños and pigeons take flight in mass formations, is that of a city that has seen everything for five centuries.

A few steps from the Plaza Mayor, the San Francisco convent is one of the most impressive colonial constructions in Latin America. Work began as early as 1535, but the complex took its current form in the 17th century after the devastating 1655 earthquake. Don't miss the catacombs: an underground labyrinth where approximately 70,000 bodies were interred between the 17th and 19th centuries, with bones arranged in geometric patterns in open wells. The library holds 20,000 volumes including some 15th-century incunabula. The guided tour lasts about 45 minutes, in Spanish or English.

In Miraflores, the Parque del Amor marks the start of the Malecón, the clifftop promenade that runs along the Pacific. The park takes its name from the large mosaic by artist Victor Delfín depicting an embracing couple. Follow the Malecón north or south to enjoy ocean views and watch paragliders launching from the cliff edge. In late afternoon, the light on the water is particularly beautiful.

activities.text: In the evening, head up to Barranco, Lima's bohemian quarter, just a few minutes by taxi from Miraflores. Cross the Bridge of Sighs (Puente de los Suspiros), a 19th-century wooden bridge that Limeños cross with their eyes closed so their wishes will come true. Then descend the Bajada de los Baños, a colorful street-art-covered alley that tumbles down to the beach, before climbing back up to Barranco's central plaza, where free concerts happen on some evenings. The neighborhood's restaurants and bars stay lively late into the night.

visits.text: The Museo Larco, in the Pueblo Libre neighborhood, is probably the finest pre-Columbian art museum in South America. Founded in 1926 by Rafael Larco Hoyle, it's housed in an 18th-century colonial mansion built atop a 7th-century pyramid. Its collection exceeds 45,000 pieces—ceramics, textiles, gold and silver—spanning 4,000 years of history. What sets it apart: its storage facility is open to the public, allowing you to wander among shelves lined with tens of thousands of ceramics. The erotic gallery, dedicated to Mochica pieces related to fertility rituals (100-800 CE), is one of the most famous in the world. Allow at least 2 hours.

In late afternoon, on an ordinary Miraflores street—between a Starbucks and a real estate agency—a 25-meter-high pyramid rises without warning. The Huaca Pucllana has been here since the 5th century, built by the Lima civilization long before the Incas. In the 1940s, real estate developers wanted to demolish it to build apartments—it wasn't excavated and protected until 1981. Today seven pyramids are visible across the six-hectare site, and archaeologists still work continuously among the adobe bricks. Go in the evening if you can (Wednesday to Sunday, 7pm to 10pm): the site is illuminated, the pyramids cut against Lima's dark sky with lit skyscrapers in the background—it's the image of this city in a single frame.

activities.text: In the morning, before the Larco museum, make a detour through the Surquillo No. 1 market, a stone's throw from Parque Kennedy in Miraflores. It's one of the best places in Lima to see the ingredients of Peruvian cuisine: dozens of potato varieties, multicolored aji peppers, exotic Amazonian fruits. It's lively, photogenic, and far less touristy than the downtown markets.

visits.text: The Ballestas Islands excursion is the highlight of the Paracas stop—book it the evening before or the morning of from the El Chaco port. Boats depart between 8am and 10:30am. En route to the islands, the pilot skirts the cliff to show the Candelabra, a 180-meter geoglyph carved into a hillside's sand, whose origin remains debated (Paracas culture, over 2,000 years ago). Then the islands draw closer and the sound show begins before the visual one: thousands of birds—Guanay cormorants, pelicans, boobies—and colonies of South American sea lions occupy every rock. The Humboldt penguin, the only penguin species in Peru, is also visible. You don't disembark on the islands, but the view from the boat is more than sufficient. Bring a windbreaker: the sea breeze is cold even in summer.

In the afternoon, head into the Paracas National Reserve, created in 1975 across 335,000 hectares of desert and coastline. Many travelers stop at the Ballestas Islands and miss what's often the best part of this stop. Stop at Playa Roja, a beach whose sand pulls toward reddish-ochre due to the presence of feldspar—the contrast with the Pacific blue is striking. Continue to the cliffs of La Catedral, a large natural arch that once jutted into the sea and collapsed in 2007 during an earthquake. The landscape—arid desert plunging into the ocean—is one of Peru's most singular.

activities.text: Arriving from Lima (~3.5 hours by road), settle in and take an evening stroll through the El Chaco village. The waterfront is lively in the evening, with several restaurants serving ceviche and fresh fish. It's a chance to eat some of Peru's best ceviche right where the fish comes out of the water.

visits.text: Huacachina is a natural lagoon formed by groundwater that surfaces in the Ica desert—the only true natural oasis in South America according to most geographers. The oasis is surrounded by palm trees and ringed by dunes reaching locally 100 meters in height. The village of 115 residents that developed around the lake was long a spa resort for wealthy Lima families (as early as the 1940s). Walk around the lagoon (about 30 minutes), then climb the crest of the nearest dune before 10am or after 4pm—the heat is intense at midday. Local legend tells of an Inca princess who, fleeing a hunter, wept and created the lake from her tears.

activities.text: The main Huacachina activity: dune buggy followed by sandboarding, in late afternoon. Operators offer outings in four-wheeled buggies (carros areneros) that tear through the dunes with an experienced driver. At stops, you descend slopes on sandboards, either standing for the more skilled or flat on your stomach for beginners. The outing typically ends at sunset from atop a dune. Count on about 2 hours. Bring sunglasses and a scarf: sand flies everywhere.

practical_info.difficulty: Easy to moderate depending on chosen slope

activities.text: A visit to a bodega in Ica, 5km from the oasis, is a lovely way to start the day. The Ica region is the birthplace of Peruvian pisco—the city has over 80 artisanal cellars. Operators offer tours with tastings of pisco sour prepared on-site.

visits.text: The Nazca Lines flyover in a small plane is the day's central activity—and the only way to truly grasp the site's scale. The geoglyphs were traced by the Nazca culture between 200 BCE and 600 CE across 500 km² of desert, by removing the layer of dark oxidized stones to reveal the lighter soil beneath. Among the most striking figures: the hummingbird (93m long), the spider, the monkey (150m), the condor, and the astronaut. Inscribed as UNESCO heritage in 1994, their function remains debated—the most solid current hypotheses link them to water-related rituals. The flight lasts 30 to 35 minutes. Warning: the plane banks alternately right and left—if you're prone to motion sickness, notify the pilot in advance. Book the day before from the Nazca aerodrome.

In the afternoon, head to the Chauchilla cemetery, about twenty kilometers from Nazca, with a local operator. This open-air necropolis in the desert was used successively by the Nazca and Wari cultures. Mummies are displayed in situ in their opened tombs—some still retain their long hair, funerary textiles, and facial expressions, thanks to the extremely dry climate. The site is impressive, sometimes disturbing, and won't suit all travelers. The preservation is remarkable across more than 1,000 years.

activities.text: If you prefer to avoid the plane flight, the alternative is the Nazca mirador, 20km north of town, from which you can observe three geoglyphs directly from the ground: the tree, the hands, and the lizard. The tracing technique—the contrast between dark stones and light soil—is clearly visible at this scale. Entry is modest.

visits.text: After nine hours on the road from Nazca—or an overnight bus waking in the dark at improbable altitudes—Arequipa first announces itself through El Misti, that perfect 5,822-meter volcano dominating the city like a stage set. Then the houses appear, all in the same cream color, nearly white: that's sillar, a porous volcanic stone that Arequipeños have been quarrying since the 16th century. The entire city is built from the lava of its own volcanoes. Drop your bags, have a coca tea for the altitude (we're at 2,330m), and take a first turn around the Plaza de Armas: it's one of the most beautiful colonial plazas on the continent, UNESCO-listed in 2000, and after the dunes and desert of recent days, its arcades and café terraces create an immediate desire to stay.

The Yanahuara mirador is a ten-minute walk from the center. You reach it via a lovely neighborhood plaza, with its church and old men on benches—and then you pass under a series of white sillar arches engraved with verses by Arequipa authors speaking of their city. On the other side of the arches: El Misti, alone in the sky, perfectly conical. In late afternoon, when the sun descends on its left, the summit gradually reddens. If you have just one photo to bring back from Arequipa, this is where to take it.

activities.text: Explore the San Lázaro quarter, Arequipa's oldest, a few steps from the Plaza de Armas. Its cobbled streets and small white sillar houses give a sense of the 16th-century city. It's also in this area that you'll find several picanterías—Arequipa's traditional restaurants, serving dishes like rocoto relleno (stuffed hot pepper) or chupe de camarones (river shrimp soup). If you arrive by overnight bus, take advantage of the afternoon to rest before tomorrow's big day.

map_keywords: Museo Santuarios Andinos Arequipa Juanita mummy

visits.text: The Santa Catalina monastery is the unmissable site in Arequipa. Founded in 1579 by Doña María de Guzmán, a wealthy widow who gave everything for its construction, this 20,000 m² (two-hectare) complex housed up to 500 nuns living entirely cut off from the outside world—until 1970, when it opened its doors to the public. It resembles a miniature village: streets with Andalusian names (Calle Córdoba, Calle Sevilla), flowered patios, fountains, private cells with walls painted red, blue, and white. Everything is built in volcanic sillar. Allow at least 2 hours, ideally starting with a guided tour (available in French), then a second self-guided visit for photos.

The Museum of Andean Sanctuaries (Museo Santuarios Andinos) houses Juanita, the 'Ice Maiden'—one of the world's best-preserved mummies. This Inca girl of approximately 12 to 14 years was discovered in September 1995 by archaeologist Johan Reinhard on the Ampato volcano (6,310m), where she had been sacrificed around 1450 during the Capacocha ritual. She's preserved in a refrigerated capsule maintained around -20°C. Note: from January to April, Juanita is placed in conservation rest and replaced by other mummies. The mandatory guided tour (about 1 hour, available in French) begins with a 20-minute documentary on the discovery expedition.

activities.text: End the day at the San Camilo market, downtown Arequipa's large covered market. Fruits, Andean vegetables, spices, local cheeses, freshly squeezed juices—this is where the city provisions itself. The city's picanterías typically serve until early afternoon: don't miss the solterito, a cold salad made with corn, fava beans, cheese, and olives that's quintessentially Arequipeño.

visits.text: The road to Chivay climbs steadily from Arequipa. After an hour, ears start ringing. Vegetation disappears. The landscape turns mineral, rust and ochre colored, with tufts of ichu—the highland Andean grass—resisting in the wind. Vicuñas trot along the roadside, indifferent to the bus. This wild camelid, slimmer and faster than a llama, nearly disappeared in the 1960s; there are now over 200,000 in the Salinas y Aguada Blanca reserve we're crossing. At the Patapampa pass (4,910m), the bus stops for a photo. You'll see apachetas—those stone towers that Andeans stack as offerings to mountain spirits. Don't linger too long: at this altitude, headaches arrive within minutes for those not yet acclimatized.

Crossing the Salinas y Aguada Blanca National Reserve, you'll surely spot free-roaming vicuñas from the bus or car. This small wild camelid, cousin to the llama and alpaca, produces one of the world's most precious wools. Pink flamingos also make regular appearances in the high-altitude lagoons.

activities.text: Upon arrival in Chivay (3,630m), after the long journey, the best remedy for fatigue and altitude sickness is a plunge into the La Calera hot springs, 3km from the village. Water heated by the Cotallumi's volcanic activity maintains a temperature of about 38°C in several outdoor pools facing the mountains. A must after hours on the road.

visits.text: Early wake-up—the Cruz del Cóndor is best appreciated between 8am and 11am, when the canyon's thermals begin rising and condors take flight. This viewpoint is about 45km west of Chivay, at the canyon's edge. The Colca Canyon reaches 3,270 meters deep at its lowest point—twice as deep as Arizona's Grand Canyon. Condors (wingspan exceeding 3 meters) often glide just meters away, in slow circles on updrafts. Arrive early: the site gets crowded and parking fills after 9am.

On the return route toward Chivay, stop in the Collagua villages of the valley. Yanque, 7km from Chivay, is the most photogenic: its 18th-century baroque church stands on a lovely plaza, and children in traditional costume often dance on the plaza around 7am. Further along, Maca is a quiet village with pre-Inca terraces still under cultivation—the same andenes as 600 years ago still irrigate the fields.

activities.text: If you're physically fit, a 2- to 4-hour hike into the canyon floor is possible from Cabanaconde. The descent to the Sangalle oasis (nicknamed Colca's 'paradise') is well-marked, accessible to walkers of average fitness, and rewarded with vertiginous landscapes. For the less athletic, a walk around Yanque along the Inca irrigation canals, on flat terrain, remains a fine alternative.

visits.text: The direct Chivay → Puno route (~270km) crosses the altiplano via a little-traveled road, between flamingo-pink high-altitude lagoons, dormant volcanoes, and llama herds. And then, at the turn of a hill, Lake Titicaca appears. At 3,812 meters, the water is a blue that has no name—not Mediterranean blue, nor alpine lake blue: something in between, intense and almost unreal under this high-altitude sky that doesn't filter the light. The lake covers 8,372 km² and extends into Bolivia. In Puno, the idea isn't to chain together visits the first evening—it's to go slowly, drink a coca tea, and watch the lake change color until sunset.

activities.text: In the evening, climb to the Kuntur Wasi mirador (15 minutes on foot from the center), where a condor statue overlooks the city. The lake view is splendid in late day. Downtown Puno is lively in the evening: several restaurants serve local Aymara cuisine, including ceviche de trucha, a lake specialty.

visits.text: The Uros floating islands are 6km from Puno port, a 30-minute boat ride. These artificial islands—between 60 and 120 depending on sources—are entirely constructed from successive layers of totora reeds that grow in the lake. The technique is ancestral: totora roots form a floating base, onto which are laid dried reed layers 80cm thick, anchored by eucalyptus poles. The lower layers rot and are constantly replaced. On site, residents explain how to build an island, how their community functions—and will offer you a short outing in a balsa, the traditional totora boat. The tourist visit is dense: if you prefer a calmer atmosphere, ask to see the Titinos islands, a less-frequented neighboring community.

Taquile Island, 2 hours by boat from Puno, lives at a rhythm hardly changed in centuries. On this island without cars or electricity (aside from a few solar panels), men have been knitting since age 8—they make the hats and textiles, while women handle weaving. Hat color indicates marital status: white for bachelors, red for married men. This textile art has been UNESCO intangible heritage since 2008. The lake views from the island's terraces, at 3,950m altitude, are among the journey's finest. The lunch served on-site—quinoa soup, grilled trout, herbal tea—is simple and delicious.

activities.text: The Uros + Taquile excursion is ideally done as a day trip from Puno port, with a local operator. Leave early (before 8am) to see the Uros in the morning, then navigate to Taquile for lunch and afternoon. Bring good walking shoes as Taquile is explored on foot via sloping trails, and sunscreen—at this altitude, the sun burns even on cloudy days.

practical_info.booking: Arrange the evening before at Puno port

visits.text: Option A - Sillustani: 34km from Puno, this pre-Columbian funerary site overlooks Lake Umayo from a peninsula. The chullpas are cylindrical funerary towers built by the Colla civilization (Aymara people dominated by the Incas in the 15th century), then enriched by the Incas themselves. The most emblematic, La Chullpa del Lagarto, stands 12 meters high with a 7.20m diameter. Remarkable feature: the towers widen toward the top, which intuitively defies gravity—it's thought this served both to showcase the masons' mastery and to reinforce the structure. The openings face east, toward the rising sun. The view of Lake Umayo and its pink flamingos is peaceful and almost dreamlike.

activities.text: Option B - Amantaní Island: the largest Peruvian island on Lake Titicaca (9 km²), less touristy than Taquile, offers homestays with local families. If you have an extra night in Puno, it's the most authentic option: dinner and breakfast with an Aymara family, ascent to the summit temples to watch the sunset over the lake.

practical_info.booking: Arrange the evening before at Puno port

activities.text: Option C - Rest and market: if the previous two days were intense, a morning at Puno's central market followed by a leisurely lunch is amply justified before tomorrow's long road to Cusco.

visits.text: The Puno → Cusco tourist bus route with stops is one of the journey's most beautiful road days. First stop: the La Raya pass (4,335m), the watershed divide between the Titicaca basin and the Amazon basin. You step off the bus into dry, sharp cold, smelling of wet earth. In one direction, the flat gray pampa extending toward Titicaca. In the other, the first slopes of the Vilcanota valley, green, descending toward Cusco. Women in colorful aprons sell wool hats and hot chicha from blankets laid on the roadside. Behind them, snow-capped peaks. Get back on the bus before altitude sickness sets in.

Raqchi is one of the least-visited and most surprising Inca sites. The temple of Wiracocha (creator god in Inca cosmology) is the only Inca building with two naves and the only one with interior columns—22 columns in stone and adobe. The central wall originally measured over 12m high. Around it, you still see the circular storehouses that held the Empire's food reserves. Allow 45 minutes on site.

The village of Andahuaylillas houses the 'Sistine Chapel of the Andes': a seemingly modest village church whose interior is entirely covered in 17th-century baroque frescoes of exceptional density and fineness. The Jesuits commissioned local artisans to paint biblical scenes with Andean faces. If you haven't seen Cusco's frescoes, this is where you'll grasp their measure.

activities.text: Arrival in Cusco in the evening. The former Inca capital, at 3,400m altitude, is one of the continent's most history-dense cities. The first evening is ideal for a relaxed stroll around the Plaza de Armas, without rushing. The illuminated facades of the cathedral and La Compañía convent are spectacular. Restaurants around the plaza serve good versions of ceviche de trucha and lomo saltado.

visits.text: The Plaza de Armas has been Cusco's heart since the Inca city's founding. The cathedral dominating it was built starting in 1559 on the foundations of Inca Viracocha's palace, with stones taken from Sacsayhuamán. Its Last Supper painted by a 17th-century Cusqueño artist is famous because Jesus and his apostles feast around a roasted guinea pig (the cuy, an Andean festive dish). Take time to enter.

The Qorikancha (Temple of the Sun) was the Inca Empire's most sacred sanctuary. Its walls were covered with 700 gold sheets torn off by Pizarro in 1533 to pay Atahualpa's ransom. The Spanish then built the Santo Domingo convent directly on its foundations. What you see today is striking: Inca walls in mortarless fitted stone—some have resisted all earthquakes—supporting the colonial church. The contrast is the symbol of all Cusco.

The San Blas quarter, on the hillside above the Plaza de Armas, is the artisans' quarter. Its narrow cobbled streets descend in staircases from the church of the same name. Enter the workshops of wood carvers, ceramicists, and weavers still working by Inca craft rules. It's also the best place in Cusco to find quality handicrafts at reasonable prices.

activities.text: The San Pedro market, 5 minutes' walk from the Plaza de Armas, is Cusco residents' daily market—not the tourist market, the real one. Fresh fruit juices pressed on-site, medicinal herbs, coca leaves, pork chicharrón, Andean breads. It's lively, aromatic, and perfectly integrated into city life. Go in the morning to see it in full animation.

visits.text: You climb from downtown Cusco on foot—30 to 40 minutes on a gentle slope—and then the walls appear suddenly, without warning. Three zigzagging terraces of blocks, some 9 meters high, some weighing up to 120 tons. No mortar, no metal, no wheel: the Incas carved these stones and hauled them here with ropes, ramps, and thousands of arms under obligatory labor law. How? We still don't know with certainty. What's certain: the Spanish quarried 80% of the stones to build Cusco below. What remains is enough to take your breath away. The fortress overlooks the entire city, and in clear weather, the view extends to the mountains closing the valley.

Near Sacsayhuamán, Q'enqo is an Inca site carved directly into a natural rock outcrop, with niches, ceremonial channels, and underground passages. It's thought to have been a site for rituals related to ancestor worship. At 9km from Cusco, Tambomachay is an Inca hydraulic site: carved stone channels continuously flow spring water into ceremonial niches. Water was sacred to the Incas, and this site beautifully illustrates that.

activities.text: The afternoon is free to enjoy Cusco at your own pace: final shopping in San Blas, a visit to the Museo de Arte Precolombino (MAP), whose collection is remarkable and premises magnificent (a 1580 colonial mansion), or simply a terrace café on the Plaza de Armas before tomorrow's big departure to the Sacred Valley.

visits.text: Chinchero, 28km from Cusco at 3,762m altitude, is known for its textiles. Women in traditional costume give demonstrations of natural dyeing and weaving—it's educational and authentic. The colonial church built on Inca foundations deserves a look, as do the surrounding ruins.

Moray is one of the most surprising Inca sites in the Sacred Valley. It's not a fortress or temple: it's an agricultural research center. Concentric circular terraces sink into the earth like a natural amphitheater, creating a 15°C temperature difference between the site's top and bottom. The Incas tested crops from different altitudes and regions here—which partly explains the 2,000 potato varieties cultivated in Peru.

The Maras salt pans are a unique visual spectacle: 3,000 terraced pools fed by a natural salt spring compose a white, ochre, and brown mosaic depending on harvest state. These salt pans have existed since before the Incas and are now cooperatively managed by local families. Annual production is 160 to 200 tons. Stop on the access road for the panoramic view of the entire site.

Pisac has Inca ruins perched on a ridge above the modern village—the ensemble is military, religious, and agricultural at once, and the terraces follow the curves with remarkable precision. The village below holds a lively market daily on the main plaza.

Ollantaytambo is one of the few Inca towns still inhabited: the village streets follow exactly the original Inca layout, with running water channels lining each street. The fortress dominating the town is built in red porphyry on monumental terraces—here Inca Manco Cápac II repelled the conquistadors in 1536, one of the rare Inca military victories against the Spanish.

activities.text: This Sacred Valley day is ideally done by taxi or private car from Cusco, with a driver who knows the sites. Cusco operators also offer guided day tours. Plan lunch at a restaurant in Urubamba, at the valley bottom, before reaching Ollantaytambo for the night.

visits.text: The Ollantaytambo fortress deserves a thorough morning visit, before groups arrive. The Temple of the Sun, atop the terraces, is unfinished—the Incas were stopped cold by the Spanish conquest. Six 50-ton red porphyry monoliths remain standing on the platform, from a quarry located across the valley. The question of their transport—across the river, then hauled up 250 meters of elevation—remains one of the great enigmas of Inca architecture. Below the fortress, the 'tired stones': blocks that workers supposedly abandoned en route during the conquest.

Pumamarka is a little-known small Inca fortress, about 1 hour's walk from Ollantaytambo. The site is little-visited and often empty—making it one of the most authentic Sacred Valley experiences. Llamas and alpacas graze freely among the ruins. The view of the surrounding mountains is excellent. The trail between Pumamarka and Ollantaytambo follows an ancient Inca irrigation canal for several kilometers—it's a section of the Inca Trail accessible to all, free, and one of the region's finest walks.

activities.text: In late afternoon, climb to the Pinkuylluna granaries on the hill overlooking Ollantaytambo to the north. These Inca storehouses perched on the cliff served to store the Empire's provisions at altitude (coolness better preserved food). The ascent takes 20 to 30 minutes. The view of the fortress from above is the best in all Ollantaytambo.

map_keywords: Aguas Calientes hot springs Machu Picchu

visits.text: The train from Ollantaytambo to Aguas Calientes (~1.5 hours) follows the Urubamba River through vegetation that changes before your eyes: from the open Andean valley, we gradually plunge into subtropical jungle. The mountain closes in, waterfalls appear, vegetation explodes in dense green. It's one of Peru's most spectacular railway panoramas. Aguas Calientes (also called Machu Picchu Pueblo), perched at 2,040m, lives exclusively for Machu Picchu.

activities.text: Afternoon and evening in Aguas Calientes are spent resting and preparing. The village's hot springs (whose name the town has adopted) are warm-water pools on the heights—ideal for relaxing muscles after previous days. Early to bed is essential: tomorrow's bus to Machu Picchu leaves as early as 5:30am.

visits.text: Machu Picchu is why hundreds of thousands come to Peru each year—and the site doesn't disappoint. The Inca citadel, built in the 15th century under Inca Pachacútec, was unknown to the Spanish. Hiram Bingham, an American explorer, 'rediscovered' it on July 24, 1911, guided by a local farmer. It was UNESCO-listed in 1983. At 2,430m altitude, the city spreads across a ridge between two peaks—Huayna Picchu and Machu Picchu ('old mountain'). The main circuit crosses the agricultural sectors, royal quarters, Temple of the Sun, Room of Three Windows, and the Intiwatana (astronomical stone). Take your time: the first hours, with mist in the valleys, are the most beautiful. Arrive at opening (6am) for a calmer citadel.

Huayna Picchu option: this steep peak (2,693m) dominating the citadel is climbed in 45 min to 1 hour via a vertiginous trail. The view from the summit, plunging onto Machu Picchu with jungle below, is spectacular. Quota limited to 400 people per day—advance booking required and often sold out months ahead. Montaña Machu Picchu option: slightly easier and less known, but equally panoramic. Same limited quota.

activities.text: The return is by bus from the site entrance to Aguas Calientes, then by train to Cusco in the afternoon or evening. The return journey takes between 3.5 and 4.5 hours total (bus + train + taxi). Dinner in Cusco in the evening—there are good restaurants on Plaza Regocijo, two steps from the Plaza de Armas.

map_keywords: Museo de Arte Precolombino MAP Cusco

visits.text: The Museo de Arte Precolombino (MAP), housed in a beautiful 1580 colonial mansion, presents a selection of the country's finest pre-Hispanic pieces—goldwork, ceramics, textiles—in soberly staged rooms. It's small but remarkably high quality, and far less crowded than Lima's Larco. Allow 1 to 1.5 hours.

The Museo Inka, in a 17th-century palace north of the Plaza de Armas, presents a comprehensive collection on Inca civilization: mummies, textiles, ceramics, everyday objects. It's one of Cusco's richest museums for understanding life in the Empire. Artisanas spin and weave in the interior courtyard.

activities.text: The last day in Cusco is also a chance to find quality souvenirs: in San Blas, artisans sell handmade pieces—ceramics, textiles, cedar wood carvings, paintings from the Cusqueño school. Hatunrumiyoc street, at the foot of the famous 12-angle stone (an Inca block fitting perfectly with eleven other stones without mortar), deserves a final pass.

visits.text: Exploring the Miraflores neighborhood and the Costa Verde promenade
Visit to Museo Larco, famous for its pre-Columbian collections
Exploring the Huaca Pucllana archaeological site
Visit to the Museo de Arte de Lima (MALI)
Discovering the LUM Museum dedicated to memory and tolerance
Final stroll through Barranco and visiting art galleries

activities.text: Last morning in Cusco before the return flight to Lima (~1h20). After three weeks spent climbing passes at 4,900 meters, watching condors from the canyon's edge, setting foot on floating islands, and touching the stones of Machu Picchu—the return to Lima by air takes on another dimension. From the window, the Andes scroll by one more time, white and impossible, before the desert coast appears and the Pacific Ocean closes the journey. If your flight time permits, a final hour in Miraflores or Barranco, one last ceviche facing the sea. Allow at least 1.5 hours to reach Jorge Chávez airport: Lima traffic plays overtime until the very end.

seo.h1_title: The Grand Southern Peru Circuit: Coast, Andes & Machu Picchu
seo.intro_paragraph: From Lima to the Ica dunes, from the Colca Canyon (3,270m deep, twice the Grand Canyon) to Lake Titicaca at 3,812m, all the way to Cusco and Machu Picchu—this 21-day circuit traverses the great Andean civilizations from north to south. It's designed for those who want everything: the desert coast, vertiginous heights, floating islands, and Inca stones that defy gravity.
seo.seo_hook: Colca Canyon and its condors, Lake Titicaca and its reed islands, Machu Picchu in the clouds—Peru's grand south, from Lima to Cusco in 21 days.
featured_list: Colca Canyon: 3,270m deep, condors within viewing range
Lake Titicaca: reed floating islands and UNESCO-listed Taquile weaving
Machu Picchu: Inca city rediscovered by Hiram Bingham in 1911
Arequipa: Santa Catalina monastery (1579), Juanita mummy, and white sillar
Nazca: geoglyphs UNESCO-listed in 1994, visible only from the air

places_taglines.PE::nazca: Desert plain where the Nazca culture carved its mysterious giant geoglyphs
places_taglines.PE::arequipa: The white city in volcanic sillar dominated by El Misti and its 16th-century monastery
places_taglines.PE::chivay: Gateway to Colca Canyon, capital of condor flight and hot springs
places_taglines.PE::puno: Lakeside city at 3,812m on Titicaca, birthplace of the Uros reed islands
